headline: Revolution in France, a republic in New York, and the year the modern age began to write itself
summary: In 1789 the political vocabulary of the modern world was rewritten in three languages at once. A bankrupt French monarchy summoned the Estates-General for the first time since 1614; by July a Parisian crowd had stormed the Bastille and the National Assembly had proclaimed the Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen. Across the Atlantic George Washington was sworn in as the first president of the United States and the new federal Congress drafted what would become the Bill of Rights. In Beijing the Qianlong Emperor entered the fifty-fourth year of his reign at the head of a Qing empire near its territorial zenith. The year linked a European ancien regime that was collapsing to an American republic that was inventing itself and to Asian and African polities still shaped by very different logics.

snapshots.text: France began 1789 in fiscal crisis and ended it in revolution. Louis XVI's finance minister Jacques Necker had convinced the king to summon the Estates-General to Versailles for the first time since 1614; elections and the writing of cahiers de doleances went on through the spring. When the Third Estate met on 5 May it soon rejected voting by order, styled itself the National Assembly on 17 June, and on 20 June, locked out of its usual hall, took the Tennis Court Oath not to disband until France had a constitution. The dismissal of Necker on 11 July inflamed Paris; on 14 July a crowd searching for arms stormed the medieval fortress-prison of the Bastille, killing its governor Bernard-Rene de Launay. A Great Fear swept the countryside in late July, and on the night of 4 August the Assembly abolished seigneurial dues and clerical tithes. The Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen followed on 26 August. In October market women marched to Versailles and brought the royal family back to Paris. Britain watched anxiously, William Pitt the Younger's ministry balancing sympathy for reform with alarm at disorder across the Channel.

The United States was inventing federal government in 1789. The Constitution ratified the previous year came into force on 4 March; the first Congress convened in New York City, then the temporary capital. On 30 April George Washington was inaugurated as the first president on the balcony of Federal Hall, taking the oath administered by Chancellor Robert Livingston. Thomas Jefferson was appointed secretary of state, Alexander Hamilton secretary of the treasury, and John Jay chief justice under the new Judiciary Act. On 25 September Congress passed twelve amendments drafted by James Madison; ten would be ratified as the Bill of Rights. To the north, British North America absorbed Loyalist refugees and reorganised under the Constitutional Act being prepared in London. In Spanish America the Bourbon reforms rolled on: silver poured from Zacatecas and Potosi, and Alexander von Humboldt was preparing the scientific expedition that would set out a decade later. In the sugar colonies of the Caribbean, particularly French Saint-Domingue, news of the Paris events spread rapidly among free people of colour and would ignite in 1791.

China in 1789 was in the fifty-fourth year of the Qianlong Emperor, a reign already the longest in Qing history. The empire stretched from the Amur to the Himalayas, and the treasury built by Qianlong's grandfather Kangxi was still, on paper, full. But the corruption of the imperial favourite Heshen was hollowing the bureaucracy from within, and the White Lotus rebellion that would erupt in 1796 was already gathering in the mountains of Hubei and Sichuan. Trade with Europe was funnelled through the Canton system; British East India Company ships loaded tea and silk at the Thirteen Factories in Guangzhou. In Vietnam 1789 was decisive: on the fifth day of the lunar new year, Nguyen Hue, the Tay Son leader who had taken the throne as Emperor Quang Trung, routed a Qing army of some 200,000 sent by Qianlong to restore the Le dynasty at the battle of Ngoc Hoi-Dong Da near Thang Long (Hanoi). In Japan the Tokugawa shogunate under Tokugawa Ienari was three years into the Kansei Reforms of Matsudaira Sadanobu.

The Indian subcontinent in 1789 was a mosaic of successor states rising out of the wreckage of Mughal authority. In Delhi the blind emperor Shah Alam II reigned under Maratha protection with little real power. In the south Tipu Sultan of Mysore, having concluded the Treaty of Mangalore with the British in 1784, was rebuilding and rearming; his invasion of the Kingdom of Travancore in late December 1789 would ignite the Third Anglo-Mysore War with Governor-General Charles Cornwallis. The Maratha Confederacy under the peshwa Sawai Madhavrao II and the powerful Sindhia and Holkar houses dominated central India. The East India Company governed Bengal, Bihar and Orissa from Calcutta under the terms of the 1784 India Act; Cornwallis was preparing what became the Permanent Settlement of 1793. In the Punjab Sikh misls competed for supremacy; the ten-year-old Ranjit Singh was still years from unifying them. In Persia the Zand dynasty in Shiraz was collapsing before the rising Qajar chieftain Agha Mohammad Khan.

The Ottoman Empire began 1789 at war with both Habsburg Austria and Romanov Russia. Sultan Abdul Hamid I died on 7 April; his nephew Selim III succeeded him at twenty-seven, inheriting the disastrous Russo-Ottoman War of 1787-1792 in which General Alexander Suvorov and Prince Grigory Potemkin were driving Ottoman armies from the north shore of the Black Sea. Selim would launch the Nizam-i Cedid, the New Order, an ambitious programme of Western-style military and administrative reform later in the reign. In Egypt Mamluk beys under Ibrahim Bey and Murad Bey ran the country as effectively independent rulers, remitting only symbolic tribute to Istanbul; French merchants in Alexandria and Cairo sent home reports that would inform Bonaparte's 1798 invasion. Along the Barbary coast the regencies of Algiers, Tunis and Tripoli lived by trade, tribute and corsairing. In Persia the last Zand ruler Lotf Ali Khan struggled against Agha Mohammad Khan Qajar, who would found the Qajar dynasty and take Tehran as capital in 1796.

In West and Central Africa 1789 marked another year of the Atlantic slave trade at its late-eighteenth-century peak: some 80,000 Africans were shipped across the Atlantic that year, mostly in British, Portuguese and French vessels sailing from the Bight of Biafra, the Bight of Benin, the Congo estuary and Angola. In London the Sierra Leone Company was preparing to resettle formerly enslaved Black Loyalists at Freetown; the Testimonies of Olaudah Equiano, whose Interesting Narrative was published in 1789, became a foundational text of British abolition. The Asante kingdom under Osei Kwame dominated the Gold Coast interior; the Sokoto jihad that Usman dan Fodio would launch in 1804 was still gathering. In Ethiopia the Zemene Mesafint, the Age of Princes, continued with the Solomonic emperor a figurehead in Gondar. In southern Africa the Cape Colony under the Dutch East India Company was pushing its frontier eastward into Xhosa country. In the Great Lakes region the kingdoms of Buganda and Bunyoro were consolidating on the northern shores of Lake Victoria.

The British penal colony at Sydney Cove was in its second year of near-starvation. Governor Arthur Phillip's First Fleet had landed 1,000 convicts and marines in January 1788; by early 1789 supplies were desperately low and rations had been cut. In April a smallpox epidemic devastated the Eora people around Port Jackson, killing perhaps half of them, an ecological catastrophe recorded in the journals of Watkin Tench and David Collins. In November Phillip captured Bennelong and Colebee to serve as intermediaries with the Aboriginal population. Across the Pacific, Kamehameha I was consolidating power on Hawai'i island, having emerged from the succession wars that followed Kalaniopuu's death in 1782; his unification of the archipelago would be completed in 1810. The mutineers of HMS Bounty had cast Lieutenant William Bligh adrift near Tofua on 28 April 1789; Bligh's open-boat voyage of 3,600 nautical miles to Timor is one of the great feats of eighteenth-century seamanship. The mutineers under Fletcher Christian sailed for Pitcairn Island, arriving in January 1790.
